sentences.text: Good morning, Mr.
Durovecz.
Good morning.
Well, I'm glad that you selected me for this-- for the purpose that you inter-- interviewing for.
My name is Andrew Durovecz-- and uh, it has been changed, the name from Gyurovecz.
It has been-- it has been modernized so- so to say to make it more like, sound like Hungarian, because originally Durovecz-- is, uh, or Dur-- that is the first part of my name, D-U-R, is French.

My grandfather was a Paris [? communat. ?] And when the uprising was suppressed by the Prussians, uh, there were a section in the Prussian army which collaborated with the revolutionaries, and they opened up-- opened up a-- a gap.
So, and several dozens of revolutionaries escaped.
And they heard about revolutions in Poland and Ukraine and Russia, and so on-- and they just took off to the east.
And landed up in a [INAUDIBLE] in the Austro-Hungarian empire.
And then they settled down there.
They didn't go any further into Poland.
They found enough comfort in [INAUDIBLE], and they became charcoal burners, and wood cutters, et cetera, et cetera.
And uh, my father was born there.
Where was your father born?
In [?
Trenchin ?] province-- I-- I forgot the name of the-- of the village.
I might remember later, but-- [LAUGHS] but then when he was-- he was at age of 13 to be ripe enough and strong enough to be an apprentice.
He had an uncle in Vienna, in the outskirts of Vienna, and he had a harness making a postal shop.
So my grandfather sent-- sent my father down as a young boy to be an apprentice with his-- with his uncle.
No, unfortunately not.
Because I never-- never saw any relatives on my father's side-- never.
And he, himself, only saw his oldest-- his oldest brother in the Italian front, where the pie-- the Py-vay-- Pi-vay River-- or Piave, as is the-- Hungarian pronounce it.
Just flipped him, it swept him away.
Andrew.
Yes.
The first son-- usually in-- in Hungarian it takes [INAUDIBLE], to the mother, and-- and the boy after the-- after the father.
Lidia Enyedi-- her second name.
Her maiden name.
Her family is from Transylvania-- where the first-- the ancient Hungarians, when they moved into the Carpathian base, there was the first settlement in that area in the present-day Transylvania.
So the first Hungarians there, it goes back to some 1,400-- 1,400 years.

Well, her father, when they moved out from Transylvania into-- uh, into Hungary, into the place in Northeastern Hungary, the province of Scabolz-- where then I was born, her father became the bellman, as they called it in the Calvinist church.

And there she was the daughter of-- of-- of that man, but they were sort of enlightened people, so to say, because the part they came from, from Transylvania, was the birthplace of the Hungarian-- Hungarian literature, Hungarian culture-- places like [NON-ENGLISH], [?
Chloe ?] they call it in Romania.
And [NON-ENGLISH]-- so that [NON-ENGLISH], and it became a family name, also.
And that-- that's how my, uh, my family-- my family name on the mother's side became Enyedi.
Oh--
Oh, that's-- [LAUGHS] almost-- almost a comical thing, then.
My father, he's the age of 21, he was a-- he was a-- how do you call this tradesman?
The-- uh, when you finish your apprenticeship, and then you become a road-- not roadman, so-something like that.
And uh, then he reached the age of 21, then he was in a military age.
And in Austro-Hungarian empire, well, everybody had to-- had to go into the empire, and you know, the empire had at least a half a dozen nationalities, or the nationals.
And he's being a harness maker, he was sent into Hungary to the great Hungarian Plains, where there were the-- the, um, cattle herds and horse ranches.
And he was sent to the stud farm there as a harness maker.
And he served his three years there-- it was compulsory.

But in every year, every spring, for about four-- four months from that central stud farm there, they sent out in the surrounding-- the surrounding area in the-- in the province, the stud farms where two or three or four depended on the-- on the agricultural area.
And he was sent out to the village, then finally where he met up with my-- with my mother.

Or to round out the story, uh, the farmers around here who were, uh, led their mares into the stud farms-- where my father was the commander in the-- in the rank of sergeant major-- and the farmers found out, and saw his work as a-- as a harness maker.
They begged him to be [INAUDIBLE].

He was going to be discharged from the army, going back to the village, and they're going to do everything they can to-- rent him a house, or give him a house, and so just open up a shop because they had no-- no harness make in the-- in the district there.
And so he did, he came back.
And he had so much work, so much work that he needed an apprentice.
And somehow, uh, the family on my mother's side found out, and there was a young boy-- the youngest boy in the family-- he became an apprentice to my father.
And he got to know my mother, and they got married.
And then, and then the first product became, that was me.
[LAUGHS]
In Kemesce-- that was the name.
Well, these names are very hard to pronounce in English, or-- or in Hebrew, or anybody else.
Hungarians are a very peculiar, complicated name.
And it was Szabolcs, it's in [NON-ENGLISH].
Szabolcs is in the northeast part of Hungary.
There is only one-- only one province, or one or two provinces between-- between Hungary and the present day Ukraine.
And at that time it was Poland and Ukraine.
There was-- there was no Ukraine, matter of fact, at that time.
Well, the village was a very large and a peculiar village.
All the wanderers in the county, many, many foreigners settled down-- like- like my father-- because the Hungarians themselves, very-- well, shall I use this term?
Backwater, in a sense, these tradesmen and-- and merchants, and so on.
And many of the merchants-- most of them, most of them-- Jewish people, you see?
Most of the merchants, and some-- some tradesmen, like the tin smith, and barber, and so on.
And, uh, among this, they used to call them the-- the aliens, and so forth.
So my father was one of them.
There was a Jewish blacksmith, and another one a Slovak this, and then a German that.
That's how they-- the trade, and the trade and commerce came about in that religion.
It, uh, it became a real-- a real highly-- highly commercialized and so on.
But the reason that it never became a town was because it was too close-- only, only 16 kilometers from the provincial-- provincial capital, [NON-ENGLISH].
So it remained a big village.
But it was a very, very advanced culture, and so on.
Right-- right in that village--
Oh, 1912-- how old you are?
19-- December 12, 1912.
So it is whenever I say it, they ask me, and so it-- it raise a smile right away.
[LAUGHS]
Yeah.
Well, it's a lot to remember, because I was-- I was two years old, already, when the war broke out, and my father was one of the very first one to be-- to be drafted.
And, uh, like most of those through Hungary army.
And the way it worked, the most backward people- like the Bosnians-- were the very first, first one in the front line, in the storming line.
And in the second, there was the Hungarian.
And in the third line were the Austrians-- that's how the Austro-Hungarian empire worked.
And I remember, very vividly, the first time about the war in 1918, when we saw the first airplane-- a French plane flew over-- over Hungary and over our village.
Because it was, as you can imagine by the, by geography.
It was close to the-- to Bosnia, toward Russian front.
And a lot of things is happened around there, a ton of troops, uh, going through there at the train line, leading through there, in my village.
And so-- and I remembered.
It was--
No, no, only at the end, end of the war, where finally the Russians army were forcing the [NON-ENGLISH] back, and then again did a team as far as the province that you come from.
You name it, now.
Marmoros. ?] Yeah, that's how I know so much about, because I heard it from my father.
And that's-- politically the war ended there.
It ended when the Austro-Hungarian army was forced back to Marmoros, to Marmoros to get in and so on the area there.
And the Count Karoly he was a count.
And he was, I don't know, own about 200,000 acres of land.
And so on, but he became the first so-called, so-called democratic revolutionary, and established the first-- first republic in October, 1918.
And then everything, everything fell apart.
It was the end of the second-- or the First World War-- at least on the part of the Austro-Hungary.
Well, I cannot remember.
The only time I can remember, back to 1918, that my-- my mother's youngest-- youngest brother got wounded on the Romanian front.
Finally, in the last-- last half year, last six months of the First World War, Austro-Hungary, eh, declared war-- war on Romania.
And my young-- young uncle, because he was really only about 17, 17 years old when he was drafted and sent out.
And he got wounded, in the [NON-ENGLISH] in Transylvania.
There, he was in a military hospital, and my mother-- my mother took me down there to-- to visit my brother.
And they were just outside a tent, laid out as wounded people.
It was so-- so bad.
Yeah, I remember that.
That was in late-- late 1918.
Well, then he was-- he was, well, the Russian front collapsed-- you see, came-- came the revolution.
And there were-- and in Russia, the-- and the war ended on the ports, and my father was with his unit, was transferred onto the-- to the Italian front.
And there, they operated again.
And in 1918, the Karoly declared, Hungary's out of the war.
And it actually was a declaration of independence from Austria.
And then the soldiers, then, yelled in-- in great joy, and someone started to throw their arms away.
And Karoly, himself-- as a rich man, a rich aristocrat-- send a message out, keep your arms, keep your arms.
Because he knew that an independent Hungary will have to defend itself against the Austrians.
And he was-- he was so right.
That was in October, 1918.
And the following year, 1919, March 21st, came the socialist revolution.
Well, they generally call it a communist revolution.
It wasn't [INAUDIBLE], but it was really a socialist revolution, with-- under the leadership of Bela Kun, who was also from Trans-- Transylvania-born.
So a Hungarian.
As I mentioned, I think the, uh, the intellectuals, and the intellectual was started from [NON-ENGLISH] from [NON-ENGLISH] and so on-- it was in Hungary at the time.
Today, it's Romania.
Well, I went to the-- went to Catholic school, because my father was Catholic.
My mother was Protestant-- Calvinist.
And, but 1918, uh, it was when I was six years old.
But being the war, and at a six class, public school was only-- only one single, one single teacher.
And you could imagine what you could get-- it was practically nothing.
We just attended a school and learned our ABC, and there what was called read anything, practically.
Because of that, in 1919, I had to repeat a year-- the first grade in the public school.
But then my father, being coming from a sort of a revolutionary family, from [INAUDIBLE] communion, and so on-- well, he was sort of a rebel, a revolutionary.
And then not to grow up in the Catholic Church, in the Catholic faith, uh, well, he was-- he was 90% atheist.
He attended the church once a year, just for the sake of [INAUDIBLE].
And then, uh, agreed with my mother and my mother [? the girls, ?] so he agreed that-- he placed me into the, for my second grade, into the Calvinist church.
My little sister, who was just-- she stayed alive in [INAUDIBLE], she is only 18 months younger than I am.
So I was-- I was in grade two, she was in great first in the Calvinist church.
And that's where I finished my, oh, the six years.
Well, then, but my father could afford it-- well, I emphasize could afford it, because very few people in those days, even to send their children into high school.
But my father, being a harness maker, in the whole-- whole district there, he was the only harness maker.
And being an upholsterer, working for the-- for the higher class, you see, so he had a little money, so he could send me to high school in the provincial capital, in [NON-ENGLISH].
And the year after that, also my sister.
And we were commuting from the-- from the village to the-- for four years, every morning, between the school.
The school term, we woke up to 5 o'clock every morning, because the train was leaving at 6.
[LAUGHS]
Well, the high schools, those days-- any kind of high schools, the my kind of, and the gymnasium-- what, the gymnasium, as they called it.
My sister went to-- and trade schools, and commercial schools-- were only lasted till 1 o'clock.
And then the train was coming, we always had to run to catch the train.
The train was leaving there [INAUDIBLE] back to the-- back to the village.
Not to the village, but on the main line.
Oh, yes, yes.
Because right there it was special-- where my- my school was, high school, it was pretty close to the-- pretty close to the station.
And there was only one streetcar line to get to the city, in the provincial city, with the population of 20,000, 25,000.
[LAUGHS] It was primitive in those days, in my time.
Berta.
Yes, I had-- I had a brother.
He was he was born in 1922.
Uh, Antal, Tony.
And another one-- another brother was older.
My sister-- my sister was born in 1914, I was born in '12, she was in 1914.
My brother, Tony, was born in 1922, and the other brother, the second, number two brother, was born in 1929.
And just before-- only less than six months before we packed up to emigrate to Canada.
And the third, number three brother, was born in 1931.
He was the only Canadian.
Canada, yeah.
Way out in the wild west-- in the west, the north, northwest Alberta.
Well, your question is very appropriate, because the northeastern part of Hungary was really heavily Jewish populated.

And my-- my province, since it was the tail end of the great Hungarian planes, and so on, it was sort of-- and during the Turkish occupation, which lasted for 160 years in Hungary, well, the main population and almost everything were almost completely destroyed.
It was a perfectly bare land, I mean, after the Turks moved out.
And needed all kinds of population, especially people with commerce and trade, and so on and so-- so forth.
The pogroms.
And then many of them started in an exodus back, and many, many of them settled [? in the ?] north-- in the north in Hungary.
Oh, yes-- and we're politically, I just counted up not very long ago.
We had the, uh, the uh, grocery store-- and the pubs and so on.
And some of the-- some of the tradesmens, and so there were all together about-- a little over 50-- between 50 and 60 in the village, like that.
Well, there were-- there were the Goldstein, there were-- there were Gross and Klein, and Klein was a small holder.
He rented about 100 acres above land, there.
And a hard part to remember-- remember the names anymore.
In Auschwitz, he was-- he was a thin maker.
And [?
Santo ?] was the-- Hungarian Jews, I have to inject this, were the most assimilated among all the Jews.
They used to claim that it was Germany-- no, it wasn't Germany.
It was Hungary.
And almost half of the of the village Jews had Hungarian names.
And many of them, well not so many converted, but the mixed marriages, eh, Christian Hungarians married into-- into Jewish girls.
There were reasons-- first of all, they were, as being merchant tradesmen, and so on-- they were well-kept, so to say.
And they were handsome, and so on.
So when I had a-- [LAUGHS] when I was in high school, my first real girlfriend was a Jew-- a Jewish girl.
Almost across the road, he was a butcher in-- in-- [INAUDIBLE], among the three.
Do you remember her name?
Ella.
And she's-- well, they, then they immigrated to Canada, settled only in Hamilton.
I, then I ran into her once-- in one kind of gathering, in a synagogue, in [INAUDIBLE].
She just came, and we looked at each other, you know we stopped and just looked and stared at each other.
And you, and you-- [LAUGHS] and that-- that was the last time I-- I met her.
She married already.
But she was a handsome-looking girl.
Oh, well, in the public school-- in the village, in the public school, uh, every one of the Jewish kids went to the Protestant church, because the Catholic church didn't a-a-accept them.
But they used to have-- used to have the Hebrew classes at the weekend, they're usually on Sundays, at-- at the synagogue there, and the-- and the rabbi's house there.
And that was the schoolhouse where they have the, uh-- well, even that-- you see, I never-- never heard, in like Yiddish, I never heard a word until I came to Canada.
Hebrew, I heard it because I mixed with the kids there, and one time, I remember, I just sneaked in, sits down in there, on the bench there, and just listened to Hebrew.
And somehow it fascinated me, and so--
Well, well, not so much, but they-- they did the teacher, you see, and they taught them Hebrew.
N-n-not very many, mind you, because it was so assimilated that the-- the Jewish, the Jewish population-- I hardly heard any-- any-- any Hebrew talks among the Jews.
They were all Hungarians.
More Hungarian then the Hungarians-- I'm not kidding that-- [LAUGHS] that that's how they lived-- lived in Hungary.
And I think it was sort of a planned way in their part to assimilate, and then be able to-- to-- to avoid persecution, and some pogroms.
And, uh, and so it happened.
And up till the day of Hitler came.
Thank you.
